meta.title: Cadence Polypulse Guides
meta.description: Short guides to read, play, and master polyrhythms with Cadence Polypulse.

index.title: Cadence Polypulse Guides
index.description: Short guides to read, play, and master polyrhythms: from the fundamentals to Coach mode.

polyrhythm-basics.title: What a polyrhythm is and how to read it
polyrhythm-basics.summary: Two rhythms at once, each with its own pulse. We show how they fit together —starting with the classic 3:2— and how to read the ring visualizer.

sections.heading: Two pulses at the same time
body: A polyrhythm is the combination of two or more rhythms sounding at once, each dividing the same span of time into a different number of pulses. In a 3:2, one voice plays 3 even pulses while the other plays 2, within the same bar. Both start together and meet again at the end of the cycle.

sections.heading: Reading a 3:2 (the hemiola)
body: The 3:2 is the most common polyrhythm and the best starting point. Picture the cycle split into 6 equal parts and count evenly.
list: The voice of 3 lands on 1, 3, and 5.
The voice of 2 lands on 1 and 4.
The "1" is the only point where both hit together.

sections.heading: The ring visualizer
body: In Cadence Polypulse each voice is a concentric ring that flashes right on its pulse. Watching the rings, you see the polyrhythm instead of only hearing it: you notice how one voice pulls ahead and falls behind the other, until they meet again at the start of the cycle.

sections.heading: How to practice it
steps: Pick a simple polyrhythm like 3:2 and a slow tempo.
Listen for a few cycles following the rings, without playing.
Add your voice: sing or tap the 3 first, then the 2.
Once they lock in, raise the tempo gradually.

coach-mode.title: Coach mode: four routines to internalize
coach-mode.summary: Beyond listening, Coach mode makes you play. Four interactive exercises measure your accuracy and track your progress.

sections.heading: What Coach mode is
body: Coach mode turns passive practice into active training: you tap on screen following a voice, and the app compares your timing against where the beats actually land. When you finish, it shows how accurate you were and saves the result to your history.

sections.heading: The four routines
list: The Anchor Shift — hold one voice as an anchor and keep the other alive in your inner ear, even after the reference audio disappears.
Ghost Audio — both voices play for a few cycles, then the audio cuts out: you keep tapping in silence and the app measures how far you drifted.
Speed Ramping — you tap the voice of the 3:2; hold your accuracy for 15 seconds and the tempo rises by 4 BPM, hunting for your "precision ceiling."
Subdivision Breakdown — first you tap the full subdivision grid, then only the cross voice, to feel the micro-grid that holds the polyrhythm together.

sections.heading: How to start
steps: Open Coach mode and pick a routine.
Set the pattern (3:2 or 4:3, for example) and the starting tempo.
Follow the on-screen prompts: each routine guides you through its phases.
When you finish, check your accuracy and try again to improve it.

sections.heading: Where to begin
body: If you're just starting out, begin with The Anchor Shift or Ghost Audio in 3:2 at a slow tempo. They're the best entry point before moving on to denser patterns like 4:3.

mixer.title: The mixer: balance each voice
mixer.summary: Independent volume, pan, mute, and solo for each rhythm. The mixer lets you spotlight the voice you're learning and place them in the stereo field.

sections.heading: A mix per voice
body: Each rhythm in the polyrhythm is a voice with its own controls. By adjusting volume and pan, you build a mix that helps you hear exactly what you need while you practice.

sections.heading: The controls
list: Volume — how loud each voice is. Turn up the one you're learning and bring the others down.
Pan — where the voice sits in the stereo field, left to right. Separating the voices to different sides helps you tell them apart.
Mute — silences a voice without removing it from the mix.
Solo — leaves only that voice playing, so you can focus on it.

sections.heading: How to use it for practice
steps: Solo the voice you want to internalize and play it until it feels solid.
Add the second voice at low volume and bring it up gradually.
Pan the voices to opposite sides to hear them separately.
Once they lock in, match the volumes and center the pan to hear the full polyrhythm.

sections.heading: No interruptions
body: You can adjust the mixer live: volume and pan changes apply on the fly, without interrupting the audio.
